body:
1) One Idea for an endpoint that can be ended is a recipe recommender in the /recipes field.  This will act as a recommender in which that all recipes with only the users allergies are given a rating score according to a predefined list of cruisine preferences that'll be added in a similar fashion as allergies.

2) Another idea is to start integrating nutrition labels,  in addition to giving a recipe id this recipe id should have the ability for the user to call the id in order to get the nutritional info of each recipe in order to implement this: nutritional info as well as quantity for both recipe ingredients and ingredients have to be implemented.
title: Product Ideas Aaron Lee
Test Results Aaron Lee

body:
1.  Delete Ingredient Should not be a post call but a delete call
2. Get Allergies should not be a post call but a get call
3. The Get Requests of get_compatible and get_ingredients should not have a body but instead be conducted in a best practice restful design otherwise /pantry/{user_id} instead of /pantry/get_ingredients
4. There are a couple of responses that end up as an array of json objects, this should be converted to singular json objects as most of your responses are singular
5.  Users should not be able to join multiple households-> handling gets messy keep it to 1 : 1.
6. Lack of quantity in pantry, this leaves a lot on the table without the ability to concurrently track ingredient usage becomes harder as a specified amount is not listed
7. Recipe Ingredients also does not have quantity, how much of a certain ingredient does a user need to use for a given recipe is unknown and important in a recipe.
8. No time stamps which makes tracking and auditing of history very hard, for example what happens if a user wants  to find the last time they used a certain ingredient or updated their allergy
9.  Ingredient Name can be listed as whitespace, this should be addressed as a ingredient can't be blank.
10. No Expiration Date on Pantry items what happens if an item spoils or what not
11. Users should have the ability to remove themselves from a household as of right now they can't, make a new endpoint to address this
12. Ingredient Names can also be listed as multiple forms, the response should be able to handle decapitalizing capitalized responses to get the true and prevent duplicates
title: Schema/API Design comments  Aaron Lee

body:
1.  Across All Files, the user exists check can be made into a separate helper function that can be imported to reduce duplication of consistently used functions.
2. In src/api/users.py, the first_name input should have more sanitized input handling so pure whitespace is not allowed. Currently, users can create a user with only whitespace such as " ".
3. In src/api/users.py, the duplicate check for allergies should inform the user of a failing allergy add instead of a non-responsive Integrity Error that does not exactly inform the user what happens. Be more intentional with what errors mean. Address this by raising an HTTP response or 200.
4. In src/api/ingredients.py, in get_all_ingredients, there is a lack of consistency in coding style when switching between SQLAlchemy and raw SQL. Use SQLAlchemy here as well if that is the coding style used throughout the project. 
5. In src/api/ingredients.py, in get_all_ingredients, it return a list of ingredients appended in a for loop; however, the ordering of the ingredient list should be consistent; therefore, within the SQL statement and ORDER BY should be used to make responses more consistent.
6. In src/api/households.py, another non-responsive Integrity Error is raised that does not exactly inform the user what happens. This time a response model is also specified yet there is not any correlated response made for the integrity error.  Address this by raising an HTTP response or 200.
7. In src/api/users.py, the email input should also be sanitized input handling so whitespace is removed and also the email is lowercased to create consistency as even uppercased letters do not make an email unique; therefore, it should apply here as well.
8. SuccessResponse is redundant as it exists in almost every file, keep one copy and import it everywhere else you need it.
9. In src/api/recipes.py, when filtering out recipes it does not explicitly check each and every ingredient rather if you have all of them singularly.  This should be handled with more intentionality and return users what ingredients are missing or rather if an ingredient does not exist with ingredients.
10. In src/api/households.py, there is a risk of a transaction rollback leaving a user not inside a household once called if the create household is rolled back. This should be handled explicitly.
11.  In src/api/ingredients.py, in delete ingredients you do not check that user exists before allowing them to delete ingredients. Make sure to add an explicit check to care for this case.
12. Overall when using ingredients from pantry if you end up using an ingredient that exist across multiple households that you have joined,  which household will the ingredient be pulled from. Also this applies to having no quantity as users have no clue how much each recipe needs of each ingredient.
title: Code Review Comments Aaron Lee

body:
# Product Ideas (Joe Croney)

Two complex endpoints that build on the existing schema. Each joins 3+ tables, involves non-trivial logic, and solves a real user problem the current API can't address.

---

## Idea 1: `GET /households/{household_id}/shopping-list`

**"What do we need to buy to make this recipe?"**

Given a household and a target recipe, return the ingredients the household is missing — i.e., the set difference between what the recipe requires and what the household's combined pantry currently holds.

### Why it's useful

Flow 2's whole premise is "minimize food waste by combining pantries." The natural follow-on question is "okay, we can almost make Tomato Basil Spaghetti-Style Rice — what are we still missing?" Right now users have to manually cross-reference `GET /recipes/get_compatible` results with `/pantry/get_ingredients` output in their head. This endpoint does that diff server-side.

### Tables joined

| Table | Role |
|---|---|
| `households` | Validate the household exists |
| `household_members` | Enumerate all users in the household |
| `pantry` | Collect every ingredient available to any member (where `is_shared_with_household = true`, plus the querying user's own items) |
| `recipe_ingredients` | Enumerate what the target recipe requires |
| `ingredients` | Resolve ingredient names for the response |
| `recipes` | Validate the recipe exists and return its name |

### Request

```
GET /households/{household_id}/shopping-list?recipe_id=4
```

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `household_id` | path `int` | The household to check pantry coverage for |
| `recipe_id` | query `int` | The recipe you want to make |

### Example request

```bash
curl -s "https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/households/1/shopping-list?recipe_id=4"
```

> Household 1 = Mark + Leo. Recipe 4 = "Tomato Basil Spaghetti-Style Rice" which requires rice (2), garlic (5), olive oil (6), tomato (7), basil (8). Assume their combined pantry holds only egg (9) and spinach (10).

### Example response (200 OK)

```json
{
  "household_id": 1,
  "household_name": "The Bachelor Pad",
  "recipe_id": 4,
  "recipe_name": "Tomato Basil Spaghetti-Style Rice",
  "have": [],
  "missing": [
    {"ingredient_id": 2,  "name": "rice"},
    {"ingredient_id": 5,  "name": "garlic"},
    {"ingredient_id": 6,  "name": "olive oil"},
    {"ingredient_id": 7,  "name": "tomato"},
    {"ingredient_id": 8,  "name": "basil"}
  ],
  "coverage_pct": 0
}
```

Or, if Mark had already saved rice and garlic:

```json
{
  "household_id": 1,
  "household_name": "The Bachelor Pad",
  "recipe_id": 4,
  "recipe_name": "Tomato Basil Spaghetti-Style Rice",
  "have": [
    {"ingredient_id": 2, "name": "rice"},
    {"ingredient_id": 5, "name": "garlic"}
  ],
  "missing": [
    {"ingredient_id": 6, "name": "olive oil"},
    {"ingredient_id": 7, "name": "tomato"},
    {"ingredient_id": 8, "name": "basil"}
  ],
  "coverage_pct": 40
}
```

### Core query sketch

```sql
-- All ingredients the household currently has
WITH household_pantry AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT p.ingredient_id
    FROM household_members hm
    JOIN pantry p ON p.user_id = hm.user_id
    WHERE hm.household_id = :household_id
      AND (p.user_id = :requesting_user_id OR p.is_shared_with_household = TRUE)
),
-- All ingredients the recipe needs
recipe_needs AS (
    SELECT ingredient_id
    FROM recipe_ingredients
    WHERE recipe_id = :recipe_id
)
SELECT
    i.ingredient_id,
    i.name,
    (hp.ingredient_id IS NOT NULL) AS already_have
FROM recipe_needs rn
JOIN ingredients i ON i.ingredient_id = rn.ingredient_id
LEFT JOIN household_pantry hp ON hp.ingredient_id = rn.ingredient_id
ORDER BY already_have DESC, i.name;
```

---

## Idea 2: `GET /users/{user_id}/top-recipes`

**"What can I almost make? Rank recipes by how much of each one I already have."**

For a given user, return every recipe in the catalog ranked by pantry coverage — the percentage of required ingredients the user has available (directly or via household), with allergen-flagged recipes excluded.

### Why it's useful

Right now `/recipes/get_compatible` only returns recipes whose ingredient list is *fully* covered. If you're one ingredient short of five different recipes, you get back nothing. This endpoint returns partial matches too, ranked so the "closest" recipes appear first. It answers the more useful question: "I have most of what I need — what should I cook tonight, and what single ingredient should I pick up?"

### Tables joined

| Table | Role |
|---|---|
| `users` | Validate user exists |
| `user_allergies` | Exclude recipes containing the user's allergens |
| `household_members` | Find all households the user belongs to |
| `pantry` | Collect available ingredients (own + shared housemates') |
| `recipe_ingredients` | Enumerate all ingredients each recipe requires |
| `recipes` | Retrieve recipe name and steps |
| `ingredients` | Resolve missing ingredient names for the "what to buy" list |

### Request

```
GET /users/{user_id}/top-recipes?limit=5
```

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `user_id` | path `int` | — | User to compute recommendations for |
| `limit` | query `int` | `5` | Max recipes to return |

### Example request

```bash
curl -s "https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/1/top-recipes?limit=3"
```

> User 1 = Sarah (peanut allergy). Pantry: chicken (1), rice (2), broccoli (3).

### Example response (200 OK)

```json
[
  {
    "recipe_id": 1,
    "recipe_name": "Chicken and Broccoli Stir-Fry",
    "coverage_pct": 100,
    "have_count": 3,
    "total_count": 3,
    "missing_ingredients": []
  },
  {
    "recipe_id": 3,
    "recipe_name": "Steamed Broccoli",
    "coverage_pct": 100,
    "have_count": 1,
    "total_count": 1,
    "missing_ingredients": []
  },
  {
    "recipe_id": 4,
    "recipe_name": "Tomato Basil Spaghetti-Style Rice",
    "coverage_pct": 20,
    "have_count": 1,
    "total_count": 5,
    "missing_ingredients": [
      {"ingredient_id": 5, "name": "garlic"},
      {"ingredient_id": 6, "name": "olive oil"},
      {"ingredient_id": 7, "name": "tomato"},
      {"ingredient_id": 8, "name": "basil"}
    ]
  }
]
```

> "Peanut Chicken" (recipe_id 2) is absent because it contains peanuts (ingredient 42), which is Sarah's allergen. "Spinach Frittata" falls below the top 3 by coverage.

### Core query sketch

```sql
WITH available AS (
    -- User's own pantry
    SELECT ingredient_id FROM pantry WHERE user_id = :user_id
    UNION
    -- Shared items from household members
    SELECT p.ingredient_id
    FROM household_members hm
    JOIN pantry p ON p.user_id = hm.user_id AND p.is_shared_with_household = TRUE
    WHERE hm.household_id IN (
        SELECT household_id FROM household_members WHERE user_id = :user_id
    )
    AND hm.user_id != :user_id
),
allergens AS (
    SELECT ingredient_id FROM user_allergies WHERE user_id = :user_id
),
recipe_coverage AS (
    SELECT
        r.recipe_id,
        r.recipe_name,
        r.recipe_steps,
        COUNT(ri.ingredient_id)                                        AS total_count,
        COUNT(ri.ingredient_id) FILTER (WHERE a.ingredient_id IS NOT NULL) AS have_count,
        ROUND(
            100.0 * COUNT(ri.ingredient_id) FILTER (WHERE a.ingredient_id IS NOT NULL)
            / NULLIF(COUNT(ri.ingredient_id), 0)
        , 0)                                                           AS coverage_pct
    FROM recipes r
    JOIN recipe_ingredients ri ON ri.recipe_id = r.recipe_id
    LEFT JOIN available a ON a.ingredient_id = ri.ingredient_id
    -- Exclude any recipe that uses one of the user's allergens
    WHERE r.recipe_id NOT IN (
        SELECT ri2.recipe_id
        FROM recipe_ingredients ri2
        JOIN allergens al ON al.ingredient_id = ri2.ingredient_id
    )
    GROUP BY r.recipe_id, r.recipe_name, r.recipe_steps
)
SELECT *
FROM recipe_coverage
ORDER BY coverage_pct DESC, recipe_name
LIMIT :limit;
```

A second pass collects the missing ingredient names (recipe ingredients not in `available`) for each returned recipe.
title: Product ideas - Joe Croney

body:
# Test Results (Joe Croney)

All commands run against `https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com`. Results below assume a freshly migrated database where the three example-flow users have not yet been created (Sarah=1, Mark=2, Leo=3, David=4, household=1). These IDs match what the team documented in `v2_manual_test_results.md`.

---

## Flow 1: The College Student with a Nut Allergy

> Sarah signs up, records her peanut allergy, adds chicken/rice/broccoli to her pantry, then queries for safe recipes. Expected: Peanut Chicken is excluded.

### Step 1 — Create Sarah's account

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/create \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"first_name": "Sarah", "email": "sarah@example.com"}'
```

**Expected response (201 Created):**
```json
{"user_id": 1}
```

### Step 2 — Record Sarah's peanut allergy (peanuts = ingredient_id 42)

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/add_allergy \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 1, "ingredient_id": 42}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 3a — Save chicken (id 1) to Sarah's pantry

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 1, "ingredient_id": 1}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 3b — Save rice (id 2)

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 1, "ingredient_id": 2}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 3c — Save broccoli (id 3)

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 1, "ingredient_id": 3}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 4 — Find compatible recipes (allergy-aware)

```bash
curl -s "https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/recipes/get_compatible?ingredient_ids=1&ingredient_ids=2&ingredient_ids=3&user_id=1"
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
[
  {
    "recipe_id": 3,
    "recipe_name": "Steamed Broccoli",
    "recipe_steps": "1) Steam broccoli 4-5 minutes. 2) Season and serve."
  },
  {
    "recipe_id": 1,
    "recipe_name": "Chicken and Broccoli Stir-Fry",
    "recipe_steps": "1) Cook rice. 2) Cook chicken in a hot pan until done. 3) Add broccoli, stir-fry until tender. 4) Serve over rice."
  }
]
```

> "Peanut Chicken" (recipe_id 2) must not appear — it requires ingredient 42 (peanuts), which is both absent from the ingredient list and flagged as an allergen for user 1.

---

## Flow 2: Roommates Minimizing Food Waste

> Mark creates a household, Leo joins, each saves one ingredient, Mark's pantry view shows both ingredients merged.

### Step 1 — Create Mark's account

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/create \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"first_name": "Mark", "email": "mark@example.com"}'
```

**Expected response (201 Created):**
```json
{"user_id": 2}
```

### Step 2 — Mark creates "The Bachelor Pad" household

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/households/create \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 2, "household_name": "The Bachelor Pad"}'
```

**Expected response (201 Created):**
```json
{"household_id": 1}
```

### Step 3 — Create Leo's account

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/create \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"first_name": "Leo", "email": "leo@example.com"}'
```

**Expected response (201 Created):**
```json
{"user_id": 3}
```

### Step 4 — Leo joins Mark's household

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/households/join \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"household_id": 1, "user_id": 3}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 5a — Mark saves eggs (id 9)

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 2, "ingredient_id": 9}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 5b — Leo saves spinach (id 10)

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 3, "ingredient_id": 10}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 6 — Mark fetches his pantry (should include Leo's spinach)

```bash
curl -s "https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/pantry/get_ingredients?user_id=2"
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
[
  {"ingredient_id": 9, "name": "egg"},
  {"ingredient_id": 10, "name": "spinach"}
]
```

> Spinach belongs to Leo's pantry. It appears here because they share a household and `is_shared_with_household` defaults to `true`.

### Step 7 — Get compatible recipes for the combined pantry

```bash
curl -s "https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/recipes/get_compatible?ingredient_ids=9&ingredient_ids=10"
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
[
  {
    "recipe_id": 6,
    "recipe_name": "Eggs and Spinach Scramble",
    "recipe_steps": "1) Beat eggs in a bowl. 2) Wilt spinach in a hot pan. 3) Pour in eggs and scramble until set."
  }
]
```

---

## Flow 3: The Aspiring Chef Updating Inventory

> David sets up a pantry with rice and chicken (representing "arborio rice" and "chicken broth"), uses them, then adds basil and tomatoes.

### Step 0a — Create David's account

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/create \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"first_name": "David", "email": "david@example.com"}'
```

**Expected response (201 Created):**
```json
{"user_id": 4}
```

### Step 0b — Seed David's pantry with rice (2) and chicken (1)

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 4, "ingredient_id": 2}'

curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 4, "ingredient_id": 1}'
```

**Both expected (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 1 — David checks his current inventory

```bash
curl -s "https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/pantry/get_ingredients?user_id=4"
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
[
  {"ingredient_id": 1, "name": "chicken"},
  {"ingredient_id": 2, "name": "rice"}
]
```

### Step 2a — Remove rice (id 2)

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/delete \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 4, "ingredient_id": 2}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 2b — Remove chicken (id 1)

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/delete \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 4, "ingredient_id": 1}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 3a — Add basil (id 8)

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 4, "ingredient_id": 8}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 3b — Add tomato (id 7)

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 4, "ingredient_id": 7}'
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
{"success": true}
```

### Step 4 — Verify David's updated pantry

```bash
curl -s "https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/pantry/get_ingredients?user_id=4"
```

**Expected response (200 OK):**
```json
[
  {"ingredient_id": 7, "name": "tomato"},
  {"ingredient_id": 8, "name": "basil"}
]
```

> Rice and chicken are gone; basil and tomato are present. Pantry reflects post-cooking, post-market state.

---

## New Test Cases

These exercise error paths and boundary conditions the example flows don't cover.

---

### Test A — `add_allergy` with a nonexistent user_id → 404

Tests that the user existence check in `users.py:54–58` fires correctly.

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/add_allergy \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 99999, "ingredient_id": 42}'
```

**Expected response (404 Not Found):**
```json
{"detail": "User not found."}
```

**Why this matters:** If the existence check is broken or missing, the endpoint would attempt to insert into `user_allergies` with a nonexistent FK and either silently fail or raise an unhandled DB error. A 404 with a clear message is the correct contract.

---

### Test B — Duplicate email on `POST /users/create` → 409

Tests the `IntegrityError` handler in `users.py:34–37`. Run Step 1 of Flow 1 a second time with the same email.

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/create \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"first_name": "Sarah", "email": "sarah@example.com"}'
```

**Expected response (409 Conflict):**
```json
{"detail": "A user with that email already exists."}
```

**Why this matters:** `users.email` has a `UNIQUE` constraint. The `IntegrityError` catch must fire and return 409 — not 500. If the handler is removed or the constraint is dropped, this returns a raw 500 from SQLAlchemy.

---

### Test C — `ingredients/delete` for an item not in the user's pantry → 404

Tests the `rowcount == 0` check in `ingredients.py:75–79`. David's pantry after Flow 3 contains only basil (8) and tomato (7). Attempting to delete rice (2) — which was already removed — should 404.

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/delete \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"user_id": 4, "ingredient_id": 2}'
```

**Expected response (404 Not Found):**
```json
{"detail": "That ingredient is not in this user's pantry."}
```

**Why this matters:** A DELETE that silently succeeds when nothing was deleted gives callers false confidence. The `rowcount` check is the only guard here — if it regresses to always returning 200, clients can't detect that their delete had no effect.
title: Test results - Joe Croney

body:
# Schema/API Design Comments Joe Croney

15 findings covering missing columns, missing tables, missing endpoints, and constraint/modeling gaps. References the actual Alembic migrations and `database.py` definitions.

---

## 1. `pantry` has no `quantity` or `unit` — inventory is binary

**Schema:** `pantry` table (`0001_initial_schema.py:58–82`)

The `pantry` table tracks whether a user *has* an ingredient, but not *how much*. There's no `quantity` (e.g., 2.0) or `unit` (e.g., "cups", "lbs") column. Every meaningful cooking question — "Do I have enough chicken for this recipe?", "I used up half my olive oil" — is unanswerable with the current schema. The compatibility check in `/recipes/get_compatible` is therefore purely set-membership (you have chicken or you don't), which is a rough approximation at best.

**Suggested columns:**
```sql
quantity   NUMERIC(10, 2),
unit       VARCHAR(50)        -- "g", "oz", "cup", "whole", etc.
```

---

## 2. `recipe_ingredients` has no `quantity` or `unit`

**Schema:** `recipe_ingredients` table (`0001_initial_schema.py:42–56`)

Recipes only know *which* ingredients are needed, not *how much*. "Peanut Chicken" needs peanuts — 1 peanut or 200g? Without quantity on the recipe side, there's no way to check whether a user's pantry holds *enough* of each ingredient even if you add quantities to `pantry`. Both sides of the relationship need quantities before amount-based compatibility is possible.

---

## 3. `pantry` has no `expiry_date` or `purchase_date` — the "minimize waste" use case is unimplementable

**Schema:** `pantry` table; **User story:** Flow 2 (`ExampleFlows.md:14–19`)

Flow 2's stated purpose is: *"roommates who hate wasting food… combine their digital pantries to find recipes that use up ingredients before they expire."* The schema has no column to record when an ingredient was purchased or when it expires. You can't query "what's about to go bad?" or sort pantry by freshness. The core value proposition of the application is not supported by the schema.

**Suggested columns:**
```sql
purchase_date  DATE,
expiry_date    DATE
```

---

## 4. No `created_at` / `updated_at` timestamps on any table

**Schema:** All six tables in `0001_initial_schema.py`, `households` and `household_members` in `0003_households_and_v2_seeds.py`

Zero tables have timestamp columns. Consequences:
- Can't sort a user's pantry by when items were added.
- Can't answer "when did this user sign up?"
- Can't audit when an allergy was recorded.
- Can't implement "recently added" or "stale pantry item" features.
- No `updated_at` means you can't tell when `is_shared_with_household` was last toggled.

`created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()` is a one-line addition to every table.

---

## 5. `users` stores only `first_name` — no `last_name`

**Schema:** `users` table (`database.py:37–43`)

```python
Column("first_name", String(100), nullable=False),
Column("email", String(255), nullable=False, unique=True),
```

There's no `last_name`. You can't distinguish "Sarah Smith" from "Sarah Jones" by name. Any household-sharing or profile-display feature immediately needs a full name.

---

## 6. No endpoint to remove an allergy

**API:** `POST /users/add_allergy` exists; no corresponding remove endpoint

You can add an allergy but never remove it. If a user was mistakenly flagged as allergic to an ingredient, or recovers from an allergy, there's no way to fix it. The inverse endpoint (`DELETE /users/{user_id}/allergies/{ingredient_id}`) is missing entirely from the spec and implementation.

---

## 7. No endpoint to leave or delete a household

**API:** `POST /households/create`, `POST /households/join` exist; no leave/delete

A user can join a household but never leave it. A household can be created but never deleted. If a roommate moves out, their pantry stays permanently merged with their former housemates' pantry. There's no `DELETE /households/{id}/members/{user_id}` or `DELETE /households/{id}`.

---

## 8. No endpoint to delete a user account

**API:** `POST /users/create` exists; no delete

A user can sign up but not delete their account. There's no `DELETE /users/{id}`.

---

## 9. No `GET /users/{id}` profile endpoint

**API:** `POST /users/create` returns `user_id`; nothing else reads user data

After creation, you get back a `user_id` integer. There's no endpoint to retrieve your own profile (name, email), list your allergies, or check your household memberships. The only user-read endpoint is `POST /users/get_allergies` (itself a REST violation — see code review #5), which returns allergy names but nothing else about the user.

---

## 10. Users cannot create recipes — catalog is static seed data

**Schema:** `recipes` table has no `created_by` column; **API:** no `POST /recipes` endpoint

The recipe catalog is seeded in migration `0002_seed_data.py` and there is no endpoint to add recipes. A user who wants to store their own recipe can't. There's no `created_by INTEGER REFERENCES users(user_id)` column, no ownership concept, and no way to distinguish "system recipes" from "user recipes."

---

## 11. No `GET /recipes` browse or search endpoint

**API:** Only `GET /recipes/get_compatible` exists

The only way to interact with recipes is to pass a list of ingredient IDs and get back compatible matches. There's no endpoint to list all recipes, search recipes by name, or retrieve a single recipe by ID. A user who wants to browse or find a specific recipe has no API surface to do so.

---

## 12. `recipe_ingredients → ingredients` uses `ondelete="RESTRICT"` — ingredient deletion is permanently blocked

**Schema:** `recipe_ingredients` table (`database.py:73–75`)

```python
ForeignKey("ingredients.ingredient_id", ondelete="RESTRICT"),
```

`RESTRICT` means Postgres will refuse to delete any ingredient row that has a reference in `recipe_ingredients`. Since every ingredient in the seed catalog is used by at least one recipe, no ingredient can ever be deleted. If you add an admin endpoint to manage the ingredient catalog, `RESTRICT` will silently block every deletion attempt with a foreign key violation. `CASCADE` or application-layer cleanup would be more appropriate.

---

## 13. No unique constraint on `households.household_name`

**Schema:** `households` table (`database.py:118–123`, `0003_households_and_v2_seeds.py:39–43`)

```python
Column("household_name", String(200), nullable=False),
```

No `unique=True`. Multiple households with the same name can be created. If two users independently create "The Bachelor Pad," there's no way to distinguish them from each other. Users joining a household by name (if that were ever an endpoint) would be ambiguous.

---

## 14. `households` has no `created_by` column

**Schema:** `households` table (`database.py:118–123`)

The household table records only `household_id` and `household_name`. There's no FK back to the user who created it. You can't enforce that only the creator can rename or delete the household. There's no concept of household ownership or management.

---

## 15. No ingredient categories, dietary flags, or tags

**Schema:** `ingredients` table (`database.py:45–50`)

All ingredients are a flat list of names. There are no categories (protein, vegetable, grain, dairy), no dietary flags (vegan, gluten-free, nut), and no tags. As a result:

- Allergy management is per-ingredient only — a nut allergy requires adding peanuts, almonds, cashews, walnuts, etc. individually. There's no way to flag "tree nuts" as a category.
- Recipe filtering can't work on dietary restrictions beyond individual-ingredient allergies.
- The ingredient catalog can't be browsed or filtered by type.

A `category VARCHAR(50)` column and/or a `ingredient_tags` junction table would enable all of this.
title: Schema/API Design comments - Joe Croney

body:
# Code Review Comments (Joe Croney)

13 specific findings. Each references the exact file and line. No generic best-practice padding — these are things that would bite a maintainer.

---

## 1. Raw `text()` SQL in `get_all_ingredients` while everything else uses SQLAlchemy Core

**File:** `src/api/ingredients.py:93–98`

```python
results = conn.execute(
    text("""
        SELECT ingredient_id, name
        FROM ingredients
    """)
)
```

Every other handler in the codebase uses the `db.ingredients` Table object (e.g., `select(db.ingredients.c.ingredient_id)`). This one endpoint drops to raw SQL for no apparent reason. If the table or column is ever renamed, Alembic will catch it for all other queries but silently miss this one. Pick one approach and use it everywhere.

---

## 2. `SuccessResponse` is copy-pasted into three separate files

**Files:** `src/api/users.py:47–48`, `src/api/ingredients.py:22–23`, `src/api/households.py:55–56`

```python
class SuccessResponse(BaseModel):
    success: bool
```

Identical class, defined three times. If you ever need to change the shape of a success response (add a `message` field, say), you have to remember to update three places. Move it to `src/api/models.py` and import from there.

---

## 3. `engine.begin()` used for read-only queries on every GET endpoint

**Files:** `src/api/pantry.py:31`, `src/api/recipes.py:55`, `src/api/users.py:95` (`get_allergies`), `src/api/ingredients.py:92` (`get_all_ingredients`)

`engine.begin()` opens a full read-write transaction that must be committed or rolled back. Read-only endpoints should use `engine.connect()`, which uses an autocommit-style connection and does not hold a transaction slot. On a Supabase free tier with a small connection pool, this silently consumes write transaction capacity on pure reads.

```python
# Current (wrong for reads)
with db.engine.begin() as conn:

# Correct for reads
with db.engine.connect() as conn:
```

---

## 4. Unreachable guard — dead code in `get_compatible`

**File:** `src/api/recipes.py:47–51`

```python
if not ingredient_ids:
    raise HTTPException(
        status_code=400,
        detail="Provide at least one ingredient_id.",
    )
```

The `Query` parameter above this (line 24) already declares `min_length=1`. FastAPI validates this before calling the function body and returns a 422 Unprocessable Entity if the list is empty. The `if not ingredient_ids` check can never be true — FastAPI would have already rejected the request. Dead code like this makes readers think there's a case worth handling when there isn't.

---

## 5. POST used for reads and deletes — REST convention violations

**Files:** `src/api/users.py:89`, `src/api/ingredients.py:65`

- `POST /users/get_allergies` retrieves data. It should be `GET /users/{user_id}/allergies`.
- `POST /ingredients/delete` deletes a pantry entry. It should be `DELETE /pantry/{ingredient_id}` (or similar).

The `get_allergies` handler even has a comment admitting the problem:

```python
"""Return the ingredient names a user has flagged as allergies.

Spec uses POST despite this being a read — we match the spec.
"""
```

Matching a bad spec rather than fixing it perpetuates the mistake.

---

## 6. Full table scan into Python memory for recipe filtering

**File:** `src/api/recipes.py:72–112`

The comment on lines 72–74 says:

> "The recipe catalog is small for V1, so this is plenty fast and far easier to read than a pure-SQL set-difference."

This loads every recipe and its full ingredient list into Python, then filters client-side. The problem:

1. As the catalog grows (even modestly — hundreds of recipes), this becomes a latency problem.
2. The `array_agg` + Python set intersection is a pattern that can be expressed as a single SQL query using `NOT EXISTS` or `EXCEPT`, which the DB can index-scan. The current approach bypasses the database's strengths entirely.

The comment treats this as a known shortcut, but the code ships to production without a follow-up ticket or TODO.

---

## 7. Existence validation copy-pasted ~8 times

**Files:** `src/api/users.py:54–58`, `src/api/users.py:60–66`, `src/api/users.py:96–100`, `src/api/households.py:28–32`, `src/api/households.py:63–67`, `src/api/ingredients.py:29–33`, `src/api/ingredients.py:35–41`, `src/api/pantry.py:32–36`

The same 4-line pattern is copy-pasted across every endpoint that needs to verify a row exists:

```python
user_exists = conn.execute(
    select(db.users.c.user_id).where(db.users.c.user_id == payload.user_id)
).first()
if not user_exists:
    raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="User not found.")
```

A one-liner helper `assert_exists(conn, table, pk_col, pk_val, label)` would eliminate all 8 copies and make the intent obvious. Right now if the 404 message or the query pattern needs to change, you have 8 places to update.

---

## 8. No logging anywhere — silently swallowed exceptions

**Files:** All files in `src/api/`, `src/database.py`

There are zero calls to `logging` anywhere in the codebase. More critically:

- `src/api/users.py:75`: `except IntegrityError: pass` — a duplicate allergy insert is silently dropped.
- `src/api/households.py:84`: same pattern for a duplicate household membership.

In production (Render + Supabase), if something goes wrong in these paths — say, an `IntegrityError` fires for an unexpected reason — you'll see no evidence of it anywhere. Structured logging at `WARNING` or `ERROR` level before the `pass` would at minimum give you a Render log entry.

---

## 9. `.env.example` referenced in error message but doesn't exist in the repo

**File:** `src/database.py:29–31`

```python
raise RuntimeError(
    "POSTGRES_URI is not set. Copy .env.example to .env and fill it in."
)
```

There is no `.env.example` file in the repository. A new developer following this error message will find nothing to copy. Either add `.env.example` with a placeholder URI or update the error message to match reality.

---

## 10. Verb in URL path; no pagination on ingredient catalog

**File:** `src/api/ingredients.py:89`

```python
@router.get("/get_all_ingredients", ...)
```

REST convention: the HTTP method is the verb. The URL should name a resource, not describe an action. This should be `GET /ingredients` not `GET /ingredients/get_all_ingredients`. The "get" is redundant.

Additionally, the endpoint returns the entire `ingredients` table with no `limit`/`offset` or cursor. Right now there are 11 rows. If the catalog grows to thousands (realistic for a real food app), every call to this endpoint returns the full set to every client.

---

## 11. Unused surrogate key `pantry_id`

**File:** `src/database.py:80–82`

```python
pantry = Table(
    "pantry",
    metadata,
    Column("pantry_id", Integer, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True),
    ...
    UniqueConstraint("user_id", "ingredient_id", name="uq_pantry_user_ingredient"),
)
```

`pantry_id` is autoincrement but:
- No foreign key in any other table references it.
- No endpoint returns it.
- No query filters or joins on it.

The `ON CONFLICT` clause in `ingredients.py:50–53` correctly uses `uq_pantry_user_ingredient` (the composite constraint), not `pantry_id`. The surrogate key adds a wasted sequence and an autoincrement column with no relational purpose. The natural key is `(user_id, ingredient_id)` — make that the primary key.

---

## 12. `array_agg or []` fallback is dead code

**File:** `src/api/recipes.py:99`

```python
needed = set(row.needed_ingredients or [])
```

The query inner-joins `recipes` with `recipe_ingredients`. Any recipe that has no rows in `recipe_ingredients` would be excluded from the result set by the inner join — it can't appear in `row` at all. `array_agg` on an inner-joined set will never return `NULL` here. The `or []` guard is unreachable and suggests a misunderstanding of how the query works. (A `LEFT JOIN` would produce NULL aggregates; an `INNER JOIN` won't.)

---

## 13. Root health check returns static data without testing DB connectivity

**File:** `src/server.py:22–27`

```python
@app.get("/", tags=["meta"])
def root() -> dict[str, str]:
    return {
        "service": "Food Graph API",
        "version": "0.2.0",
        "docs": "/docs",
    }
```

Render uses this endpoint (or `/`) as a health check. If the Supabase connection is dead, this endpoint still returns `200 OK` because it never touches the database. Render will believe the service is healthy and route traffic to a broken instance. A minimal health check should run a cheap query (e.g., `SELECT 1`) and return 503 if it fails.
title: Code Review Comments - Joe Croney

body:
1. Should add a created_at or saved_at field in the pantry so that it is easier to debug and you can keep track of how long a ingredient has been in the pantry.

2. It might be easier to have a household pantry for each household which is automatically added to if an ingredient has is_shared_with_household true.

3. Add a quantity field to pantry so users can input how much of an ingredient they have.

4. Add a quantity field to recipe_ingredients as well so that recipes only use a certain amount of ingredients.
**3 and 4 would be especially useful for meal planning, but may also be useful in general.**

5. Allow users to add ingredients by ingredient name rather than needing to always know the id. 

6. Get Compatible Recipes should maybe have a short description explaining why it was chosen and what ingredients it uses from the ingredient list / pantry.

7. As the API is set up currently, there is no API token needed to access the endpoints, which is a security vulnerability.

8. As the API is set up currently, a user can change the pantries, households, etc for any other user. This can also be a security vulnerability. Maybe include a LOGIN endpoint that returns a unique token that the user then needs to use to access the endpoints.

9. Where success = true is returned, add a brief success description message so that the user can confirm that everything went OK.

10. Nitpick: Lowercase/Normalize the emails before adding them so that A@gmail.com and a@gmail.com are not both accepted. You would probably want a unique_constraint_violation to be thrown in that case.

11. Add a remove from household endpoint to remove someone from a household. This should also stop them from having access to shared household items (this would be easy with a houshold pantry)

12. Add a added_at field to housholds when a user joins a household for a similar reason to #1.
title: Schema/API Design Comments (Anay)

body:
1. https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/households.py#L77-L86

If the user is already a member of the household, the API should show a message saying that the user is already a member. Also, there is no point at which success = False is ever used, so maybe you could use it here. While technically the user is in the household in the database, it can be useful to inform them that their endpoint call failed and why.

2. https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/ingredients.py#L27

It might be more user-friendly to be able to save ingredients by the name rather than the id. Otherwise, users would need to search for their ingredient id every time. Same with Delete Ingredient

3. 
https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/pantry.py#L49-L71

So this also shows ingredients in a pantry of a housemates. This could mislead a user if they want to remove it after because they see that they have it. Maybe either add shared items to the pantries of all household members when saving it or split the output of this into My Pantry and Shared Pantry.

4. 
https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/pantry.py#L60-L68

This query is not easily readable. You could split the query into My Pantry and Shared Pantry and then OR them at the end.

5.
https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/recipes.py#L23-L39

This endpoint seems to always need the ingeredient ids passed in. What if the user just wants to use their pantry as the ingredients? It would be nice to add a path where if they don't add ingredient ids, it would automatically use their pantry. May need to make user_id required.

6.
https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/recipes.py#L75-L95

Maybe add an ORDER BY so that the results are in a predictable order every time. Or add a ranking system so that the recipes are ranked when they are outputted.

7.
https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/recipes.py#L75-L95

While you did mention that this is OK for V1, it would be good to filter in SQL as the recipe catalog grows. From my testing, the recipe catalog isn't too big yet, so you might want to add some more recipes.

8.
https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/users.py#L68-L78

Similar thing as #1

9.
https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/users.py#L16

Here someone can input all whitespace "        ", and I believe it would allow it as a first name. This may break something in Postgres or at least make it look weird.

10.
https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/users.py#L54-L58

Since you are checking whether the user exists in almost every WRITE/POST endpoint, it would be best to create a helper function somewhere and call that.

11.
https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/ingredients.py#L67-L73

Check if the user_id and ingredient_id in general exist first similar to what you do in save_ingredient. Your error message should be different in those cases.

12.
https://github.com/moystee/CSC365_Database_Group_Project/blob/031e2c14d108c6280b010caf3fd12d771576c7d0/src/api/users.py#L90

You should add a remove allergy endpoint so that someone can remove their allergy if they added the wrong one or got miraculously cured.
title: Code Review Comments (Anay)

body:
1. Weekly Meal Plan Endpoint

This endpoint could create a meal plan for 7 days (or maybe however many days the user specifies) based on their allergies and the ingredients in their pantry/household. To help with this, when the user is saving an ingredient, they could input how much of that ingredient they have (e.g. 1 cup, 500g) and the endpoint could then use the ingredient over multiple recipes (but not overuse an ingredient). At the end of the 7 days, the user could then remove those ingredients.

2. Get Recipes for Full Household

This endpoint could find recipes that work for the whole household rather than just one person. It can take only ingredients that are shared between all members of the household and take into account allergies for each person in the household. It could then rank the recipes based on how well they use all the ingredients. This would help people in the same household not have to make different meals.
title: Product Ideas (Anay)

body:
# Running Through Example Workflows

## 1. College Student with a Nut Allergy

1.1 Create Account
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/create' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "first_name": "anay",
  "email": "anay@testing.com"
}'
```
Response:
{"user_id": 8}

1.2 Record Allergy to Spinach
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/add_allergy' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 8,
  "ingredient_id": 10
}'
```
Response:
{"success": true}

1.3a Save chicken to pantry
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 8,
  "ingredient_id": 1,
  "is_shared_with_household": true
}'
```
Response:
{"success": true}

1.3b Save rice to pantry
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 8,
  "ingredient_id": 2,
  "is_shared_with_household": true
}'
```
Response: 
{"success": true}

1.3c Save broccoli to pantry
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 8,
  "ingredient_id": 3,
  "is_shared_with_household": true
}'
```
Response:
{"success": true}

1.4 Find Compatible Recipes
```
curl -X 'GET' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/recipes/get_compatible?ingredient_ids=1&ingredient_ids=2&ingredient_ids=3&user_id=8' \
  -H 'accept: application/json'
```
Response:
[
  {
    "recipe_id": 3,
    "recipe_name": "Steamed Broccoli",
    "recipe_steps": "1) Steam broccoli 4-5 minutes. 2) Season and serve."
  },
  {
    "recipe_id": 1,
    "recipe_name": "Chicken and Broccoli Stir-Fry",
    "recipe_steps": "1) Cook rice. 2) Cook chicken in a hot pan until done. 3) Add broccoli, stir-fry until tender. 4) Serve over rice."
  }
]

## 2. Roommates Minimizing Food Waste

2.1 Account already created in Flow 1

2.2 Create a household
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/households/create' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 8,
  "household_name": "Anay'\''s Household"
}'
```
Response:
{"household_id": 2}

2.3 Create Andy's Account
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/create' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "first_name": "Andy",
  "email": "andy@gmail.com"
}'
```
Response:
{"user_id": 9}

2.4 Andy joins Anay's Household
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/households/join' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "household_id": 2,
  "user_id": 9
}'
```
Response:
{"success": true}

2.5a Anay saves eggs to his pantry
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 8,
  "ingredient_id": 9,
  "is_shared_with_household": true
}'
```
Response:
{"success": true}

2.5b Andy saves spinach to his pantry
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 9,
  "ingredient_id": 10,
  "is_shared_with_household": true
}'
```
Response:
{"success": true}

2.6 Anay checks his pantry
```
curl -X 'GET' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/pantry/get_ingredients?user_id=8' \
  -H 'accept: application/json'
```
Response:
[
  {
    "ingredient_id": 1,
    "name": "chicken"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 2,
    "name": "rice"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 3,
    "name": "broccoli"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 9,
    "name": "egg"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 10,
    "name": "spinach"
  }
]

2.7 Get compatible recipes from both pantries (one household)
```
curl -X 'GET' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/recipes/get_compatible?ingredient_ids=9&ingredient_ids=10&user_id=8' \
  -H 'accept: application/json'
```
Response:
[] (good because i have a spinach allergy)

## 3. Updating inventory

3.1 Check current inventory
```
curl -X 'GET' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/pantry/get_ingredients?user_id=8' \
  -H 'accept: application/json'
```
Response:
[
  {
    "ingredient_id": 1,
    "name": "chicken"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 2,
    "name": "rice"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 3,
    "name": "broccoli"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 9,
    "name": "egg"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 10,
    "name": "spinach"
  }
]

3.2a Remove spinach
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/delete' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 8,
  "ingredient_id": 10
}'
```
Response:
{
  "detail": "That ingredient is not in this user's pantry."
}
**The ingredient should be in my pantry as I checked my pantry in the previous step and ingredient 10 was in there.**

3.2b Remove rice
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/delete' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 8,
  "ingredient_id": 2
}'
```
Response:
{"success": true}

3.3a Add fresh basil to pantry
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 8,
  "ingredient_id": 8,
  "is_shared_with_household": true
}'
```
Response:
{"success": true}

3.3b Add tomatoes to pantry
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/ingredients/save' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "user_id": 8,
  "ingredient_id": 7,
  "is_shared_with_household": true
}'
```
Response:
{"success": true}

3.4 Get pantry again
```
curl -X 'GET' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/pantry/get_ingredients?user_id=8' \
  -H 'accept: application/json'
```
Response:
[
  {
    "ingredient_id": 1,
    "name": "chicken"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 3,
    "name": "broccoli"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 7,
    "name": "tomato"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 8,
    "name": "basil"
  },
  {
    "ingredient_id": 9,
    "name": "egg"
  },
  {

    "name": "spinach"
  }
]

## Summary
The only one that failed was 3.2a. It could be because spinach came from the household and not directly from his pantry.

# New Test Cases

1. Creating an account with an invalid email.
```
curl -X 'POST' \
  'https://foodgraph-api.onrender.com/users/create' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "first_name": "InvalidEmailTest",
  "email": "not-an-email@"
}'
```

        "email"

      "msg": "value is not a valid email address: There must be something after the @-sign.",
      "input": "not-an-email@",
      "ctx": {
        "reason": "There must be something after the @-sign."

}
